result:
There was once a city built on the exact hinge between waking and sleep. During the day its streets hummed with ordinary commerce: sweat, engines, ringing copper coins. At night, however, a second population unlatched the walls from inside, slid panels open like enormous eyes, and stepped out into time that nobody had properly catalogued. They called this place Somnopolis, and they believed, without much evidence, that if they looked hard enough they could see tomorrow trying on a different face in the mirror.

As a child, I was allowed to run errands between the two jurisdictions. Day turned me out like a coin into someone’s palm; night waited at the corner, cloaked in ultraviolet, to fold me back into its sleeve. I learned to read both schedules. My mother worked inside the hourglass-shaped archive whose bottom bulb was planted in concrete while its top evaporated somewhere above the roofline. She said the point was not to keep records straight but to prove that gravity and levity agreed on a secret treaty: what falls on one side rises on the other, plus interest. She calibrated the exchange rate by trimming columns of unwanted memories—bad birthdays, tiresome tsars, minor wars—until the scale balanced. Then she floated the surplus toward the ceiling as bright lantern-birds that nested in the rafters, their wings humming like neon disbelief.

Runners like me carried pigeon-sized pouches from the underside to the upside. Each pouch contained a single syllable of the city’s true name. Once a year, 3,127 syllables were tossed skyward in one eruption of feathers, and the resulting chord, half word and half weather, reminded every sleeper why insomnia kept inventing strangers for us to love. Those who heard it woke momentarily in two beds at once: the one they owned and the one that had been prepared for them in the next life. For a breath, the mattresses overlapped like Venn diagrams. Their pulses counted one another off like metronomes seeking common time.

I was seventeen and impressionable when the percussion stopped. A censorship board, composed mostly of alarm clocks and skeptical traffic lights, argued that uncertainty harmed civic morale. Overnight they built a thin, self-important fence between the two cities, made entirely of square seconds sawn off the edges of minutes. Its posts clicked with a sound that suggested time was nailing itself shut. Anyone reaching the boundary felt an abrupt subtraction: memories older than breakfast vanished in patches. Runners collapsed mid-dash, their pouches spilling backward into words onto the pavement—E-, un-, -riv-, -erse—raw letters wriggling like nicked vowels bleeding semantic plasma. We tried collecting them but many expired in the heat of the following noon, becoming just asphalt freckles that sang quietly when stepped on.

My mother attempted diplomacy. She ironed her official robe, lint-rolled the ancestral despair from its sleeves, and marched to Customs Hall carrying a briefcase overstuffed with inverted commas. Her plea was simple: release the syllables so the chord could still convene. The board filed her petition between Section 41-B for Fog and Section 42-C for Fervor. Every hour she returned; every hour the briefcase grew lighter, commas evaporating like parentheses turning conspiratorial. After the twenty-third visit she noticed a small rent in the sky shaped like a dollar sign leaking either dusk or dawn. By the twenty-fourth, the rent had scaled its own lips shut.

I waited beside the fence each evening, ears pressed to where minutes used to enter seconds, hoping to overhear the racket of resurrection. Instead I accumulated my own missing parts: the memory of my first bicycle arc, the taste of August figs laced with seawater, the way my father’s voice once hinged open to scatter homing stars across the ceiling. One by one they lifted off my shoulders and printed themselves onto the fence until I was fluent only in the white space between debts. Loss calcified into something adjacent to courage. I decided to erase the fence by forgetting it hard enough that even the fence would doubt its edges.

For three consecutive midnights I committed a brand new crime: I stayed continuously awake. I wandered the day streets wearing every minute I had stolen, tied in flamboyant bows around my calves, until the exasperated sun overexposed the sidewalks hoping to lodge me like film grain into permanent noon. I laughed in over-bright doorways until shadows filed noise complaints. On the fourth night, exhausted, I crept to the hourglass building now dimmed and gated. Its upper bulb had completely detached and hovered two blocks west, wobbling like a jellyfish that had misread the moon. I climbed the zigzag ladder the caretaker used to feed abandoned equations to beasts of pure density. At the top, instead of balance pans, I found my mother seated inside the jellyfish bulb, legs crossed, measuring feathers with a stopwatch.

She wasn’t alone. Around her orbited slight, translucent duplicates of everyone who had ever carried a syllable: younger versions of us, older versions of those, flickering in and out as the bulb drew on the city’s latent insomnia for momentum. Her hands trembled but did not stop timing. In her lap lay the final pouch, already open like the sweetest small mouth invited to make one last confession. The chord’s final syllable, a silver bar of sound, lay across her palms humming its tie to the room’s atmosphere. It sounded like the very first hush ever exchanged between a mother and an infant, universal but freshly coined.

I spoke. In place of words I borrowed the hush and nestled it into her eyebrows. Recognition dawned behind her pupils like an eclipse remembering its first taste of sunlight. She stood, cradling the syllable as though it was a salt bird soon to melt. Together we walked up a crooked staircase of air until we stood against the rent in the sky. There we did the simplest thing: we fed the unpronounceable name back into the rift. The fence below recoiled like a tape measure ashamed of its own length; seconds spilled outward like caviar reclaimed by the tide.

The chord rang out—not triumphal but provisional, a reminder that balance is an agreement renewed nightly rather than a trophy to weigh. Every missing memory slid down the sky’s repaired seam and re-entered me with soft thuds, like library cards returning themselves. The fence dissolved into scissor scars that closed almost politely. Citizens felt the seam settle; dreams arrived on time for the first time in years; even the traffic lights conceded an amber wink.

In the aftermath the city installed a modest park where the fence had stood. It contains one bench, one fountain, and an explanation plaque that evaporates upon reading. On the anniversary of the rent’s closing, runners still leave candles of condensed sleep around the fountain. Their flames swirl counterclockwise to commemorate all former detours. My mother and I uncork vials of unspent insomnia and let them drift upward until the hush rematerializes, tinged with distant earthquake, small invisible bells.

Occasionally I think I might still be seventeen, perpetually on the ladder, eternally offering the final syllable to a closed sky. Other nights I wake to find my pillow slightly damp—as if memory, arriving again, cannot decide whether to be dew or tear—and I accept the residue as proof that the city continues breathing on both sides of itself, lips moist with tomorrow’s weather. Somnopolis remains half awake, half asleep, its heart beat divided cleanly between two polite conspiracies: remembering long enough to let go, and forgetting just in time to remember again.
